segments.s019-g145: This is a prepositional phrase plus a relative clause: 'به یزدان' marks the target/recipient of ingratitude ('to/against God'). 'هر آن کس که شد ناسپاس' is a free relative 'whoever became ungrateful' — noun phrase ('هر آن کس') + relative particle 'که' + past of شدن ('شد') + predicate adjective 'ناسپاس'. Word order is typical of classical Persian poetry: the verb ('شد') follows the relative and precedes the adjective that completes the predicate.
segments.s019-g146: 'به دلش اندر آید' literally 'into his heart comes' — the prepositional phrase 'به دلش' ('into his heart', with the clitic 'ش' = his) precedes the verb 'آید' (3sg present/simple 'comes'). 'اندر' reinforces the locative 'in/within'. 'ز هر سو هراس' gives the source: 'from every side, fear' (z = from, هر سو = every direction). The line uses a V‑initial order (verb before its nominal complement) common in poetic diction.
segments.s019-g147: 'به جمشید' is a dative/experiencer phrase ('for Jamshid'). The clause 'بر تیره‌گون گشت روز' literally 'the day became dark‑hued' — here 'بر' + adjective 'تیره‌گون' marks the change/state and 'گشت' (past of شدن) is the verb 'became/turned', placed before its subject 'روز' in poetic word order.
segments.s019-g148: 'همی کاست' is a poetic imperfective or iterative past sense: the adverb 'همی' gives continuative force ('kept on / continually'), and 'کاست' is the past form of کاستن ('diminished/waned'). The phrase 'آن فر گیتی‌فروز' is the subject/obj phrase 'that royal glory, world‑illuminating' (فر = royal glory; گیتی‌فروز an epithet). The verb precedes the noun, so the sense is 'that world‑illuminating glory was continually decreasing.'
segments.s019-g57: چهارم is an ordinal, 'the fourth' or 'fourthly' and introduces an item in a list. که خوانند introduces a relative/clause 'which they call'; خوانند is a classical third-person plural present/present-subjunctive form meaning 'they call' (used to name something). اهتوخوشی is a struck, somewhat obscure noun quoted as the name they give.
segments.s019-g58: همان = 'that/the same' (pointing back to the named thing). دست‌ورزان is a plural nomitive/genitive noun 'manual workers / artisans'. ابا here functions as a noun 'reluctance/refusal' (not a verb) and is linked to سرکشی, which names what they are reluctant about; سرکشی = 'rebellion/defiance'. The whole phrase reads 'the very artisans' reluctance to rebel.'
segments.s019-g59: کجا is used adverbially as 'where.' کارشان = 'their work' (کار + possessive شان). همگنان پیشه is a compound meaning 'the craft/profession of their peers' (همگنان 'fellows/peers' modifying پیشه 'profession/craft'). بود is the past copula 'was' completing the clause. Word order is typical classical Persian: locative/adverbial + subject + predicate + بود.
segments.s019-g60: روانشان = 'their mind/spirit' (روان + possessiveشان). همیشه = 'always.' پر اندیشه is a poetic adjectival phrase 'full of thought' (پر = 'full (of)'; اندیشه = 'thought'). بود is again the past copula 'was.' So the clause states their minds were constantly thoughtful.
segments.s019-g61: بدین here functions as the fused prepositional phrase 'in this way / thus' (formally بِ + این). اندرون = 'within / inside' and سال پنجاه = 'fifty years' (a duration). نیز = 'also.' Together they form a temporal/locative phrase 'thus, within fifty years as well.'
segments.s019-g62: A sequence of past-tense verbs describing actions: بخورد (here past 'he ate' or 'he consumed'), بورزید (past of ورزیدن 'he practiced/worked/exerted himself'), بخشید (past 'he gave/bestowed'); the final noun چیز 'a thing / things' follows the verbs and functions as the object (poetic word order places object after verbs). The conjunction و links the verbs.
segments.s019-g63: از این = 'from these / of these.' هر یکی را = 'each one' with را marking the following phrase as definite/objective. یکی پایگاه = 'one station/base' (یکی = 'one / a certain' modifying پایگاه). The phrase as a whole sets up a partitive: 'from these, each one (a) base...' awaiting the verb.
segments.s019-g64: سزاوار = 'appropriate / fitting' qualifying the chosen base. بگزید is past 'he chose/selected' (گزیدن). و بنمود links the next verb 'and showed' (بنمود = 'he displayed/pointed out'). راه = 'the way / path', the object of بنمود. The clause therefore says 'each chose a fitting station and showed the way.'
segments.s019-g113: ز ... ز introduces two ablative phrases ('from / because of'); رنج و ز بدشان is a coordinated genitive/possessive phrase ('their suffering and their bad / misfortune'). نبد is an older negative copula/past-form (cf. نبود) meaning 'was not / did not exist' and takes آگهی (information/notice) as its subject: 'no notice existed.'
segments.s019-g114: میان 'in the middle / between' qualifies the following predicate; بسته is a past participle/adjectival predicate 'closed/shut' modifying دیوان (the court/office). به سان + رهی is a simile 'like a wayfarer' (رهی = wayfarer), so the whole clause reads 'the divan was shut in the middle like a wayfarer.'
segments.s019-g115: به فرمان مردم is an agent/instrumental-like prepositional phrase 'by (or at) the command of the people.' نهاده is a past participle/verb 'placed, set' and appears before its object دو گوش ('two ears' = both ears) — an idiomatic phrasing meaning 'he lent his ears / listened to the people.'
segments.s019-g116: ز رامش جهان پر uses two z-phrases to enumerate causes/contents: 'ز رامش جهان' = 'of the world's tranquillity' and پر ز آوای نوش = 'full of the sound of delight.' پر (here following جهان) functions as 'full (of)' and ز + noun marks the content/source.
segments.s019-g117: چنین تا introduces a temporal clause 'thus until ...'. بر آمد is the verbal phrase (برآمد) 'came / befell' and is placed before the locative/time phrase بر این روزگار 'upon this age / at this time' — a slightly archaic word order typical of epic verse.
segments.s019-g118: ندیدند is simple past, 3rd-person plural 'they did not see.' جز is the exception marker 'except,' followed by خوبی 'goodness' and از کردگار 'from the Creator' — together: 'they saw nothing except goodness from the Creator.'
segments.s019-g119: جهان سربه‌سر گشت uses سربه‌سر as an adverb 'entirely / altogether' modifying گشت (گشت = 'became, turned into'). او را رهی is a somewhat inverted expression: را marks the affected person (him) and رهی ('a road, path, wayfarer') functions as the predicate complement — 'the world became wholly a road/path for him.'
segments.s019-g120: نشسته is a participial/past form 'seated / sat,' and جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign.' با introduces a circumstantial phrase; فرهی is a poetic noun meaning 'nobility / grandeur' — 'the sovereign sat with nobility.'
segments.s019-g25: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking the material source. The nouns follow the typical Persian coordinative sequence with و 'and' linking items: کتان (linen), ابریشم (silk) and موی قز (literally 'hair of the qez/silkworm' = raw silk/hair). The genitive relation موی قز is a simple noun + noun construct: موی (hair) governed by قز (silkworm/raw silk).
segments.s019-g26: کرد is the past tense 'made/did' with قصب as its direct object phrase; here the clause means 'he made/turned into (قصب)'. پر مایه is a compound adjective: پر 'full (of)' + مایه 'substance/wealth' giving 'richly endowed' or 'substantial'. دیبا و خز are the two resulting textile items ('brocade and velvet/fur') linked by و. Note: قصب here is an archaic/poetic noun whose specific technical sense (a kind of spun/stem/thread) is not represented in the provided sense list.
segments.s019-g27: بیاموختشان is a past transitive verb 'he taught them' (بی‑ prefix + آموخت + object suffix -شان). The following verbs رشتن and تافتن are infinitives naming the skills taught: رشتن 'to spin' (make threads) and تافتن is a specialized textile verb here; its precise technical sense is poetic/archaic and not matched in the provided sense list.
segments.s019-g28: This is an infinitival phrase introduced by به meaning 'into/onto' (directional here): به تار اندرون پود را بافتن = 'to weave the weft into the inner warp.' تار 'warp' and پود 'weft' are technical weaving terms; اندرون 'inside/within' modifies تار, and را is the definite object marker attached to پود; بافتن is the infinitive 'to weave.'
segments.s019-g29: چو شد introduces a temporal clause 'when it happened/when it became'. بافته is a past participle/adjectival form 'woven' describing the cloth, and the following infinitives شستن و دوختن list the subsequent actions 'washing and sewing.' The clause reads 'when it was woven, (they did) washing and sewing.'
segments.s019-g30: گرفتند here is a past tense 'they took' used idiomatically with an infinitive to mean 'they took up / began' (گرفتند ... آموختن = 'they began to learn'). از او 'from him' marks the source/teacher, یک‌سر is an adverb meaning 'completely/at once', and آموختن is the infinitive 'to learn.' The whole says they immediately learned everything from him.
segments.s019-g31: چو این کرده شد is a temporal 'when this had been done.' ساز دیگر is 'another device/method' (ساز used here as 'means/device'), and نهاد is the past tense of نهادن 'he placed/put (in place)' — so the clause means 'when this was done, he put/established another device.'
segments.s019-g32: زمانه functions here as 'fortune/the times' (an impersonal subject). بدو is the dative 'to him' and شاد is the predicate adjective 'was glad/merry' — idiomatically 'the times smiled on him' (i.e. fortune favored him). The following conjunction و links the rest: او نیز شاد 'and he also was glad.'
segments.s019-g65: که تا together introduce a purposive clause ('so that' / 'in order that'); هر کس is the indefinite subject 'each person'; اندازه خویش را is a definite object phrase: اندازه 'measure' modified by the possessive suffix ش ('his/own'), and را marks it as definite/object. Word order is classical Persian: the purpose marker comes first, the subject follows, and the object is fronted and marked with را before the verbs that complete the clause.
segments.s019-g66: ببیند and بداند are third‑person singular present/subjunctive forms used in a purposive/optative sense ('may see' / 'may know') governed by که تا; the two verbs are coordinated so the subject (هر کس) applies to both. کم و بیش is a fixed binary phrase meaning 'lack and excess' or 'less and more' and را (here) makes that phrase definite/the object of بداند.
segments.s019-g67: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'; پس 'then' is a temporal/sequence adverb. دیو ناپاک را is the patient/accusative (the demon being ordered) with را marking the object of the command. In classical Persian an object marked with را can be the indirect object of a following infinitival clause (the one to be commanded).
segments.s019-g68: آمیختن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to mix' and functions as the action commanded. به ... اندر gives the direction/locative 'into' (به آب اندر = 'into the water'), خاک را is the direct object of آمیختن and is marked with را. The whole phrase therefore expresses 'to mix the earth into the water' as the commanded action performed by the demon.
segments.s019-g69: هر آنچ از گل آمد is a free relative 'whatever came from the clay'; آنچ = 'that which/whatever', از marks source 'from', گل here means 'clay/mud' (not flower), and آمد is the past verb 'came'. چو introduces the temporal subordinate clause 'when' and بشناختند is the past plural 'they recognized/identified'.
segments.s019-g70: سبک خشت را is the definite object phrase 'the light brick(s)' (سبک as adjective 'light' modifying خشت 'mudbrick'), with را marking that object; کالبد ساختند is the verbal phrase 'they fashioned/made a body/form' (کالبد 'form/body' + ساختند 'they made'). In effect the clause says they shaped the recognized clay into light bricks.
segments.s019-g71: به سنگ و به گچ here uses به in an instrumental/material sense 'with' or 'of' (i.e. made of stone and of plaster); دیو is the subject 'the demon', دیوار is the object 'walls', and کرد is the past verb 'made/ did' — together: 'the demon made/erected walls of stone and plaster.'
segments.s019-g72: نخست 'first/at first' sets temporal order; از before برش هندسی here has a partitive/templating sense ('beginning with' or 'as for'), and برش هندسی is a noun phrase 'geometric cutting/carving' (هندسی postmodifies برش). کار کرد is the past verb phrase 'he worked/did work', so the clause means 'first he worked at geometric cutting.'
segments.s019-g97: که is the relative/connecting particle 'that' linking this clause to the preceding statement. چون here functions temporally as 'when' (classical usage) introducing the time clause. خواستی is the 2nd-person singular past of خواستن ('you wished/desired'); in narrative it marks a completed past desire or moment. دیو is the direct object 'a demon,' and برداشتی is the 2nd-person singular past of برداشتن ('you took up/took away'); the verb sequence makes the action performed when you wished.
segments.s019-g98: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' هامون names the plain/marshland source ('the plain'). به here marks direction 'to/into' and combines with گردون, which is a poetic word for 'the sky/heaven.' برافراشتی is the past 2nd-person form of برافراشتن 'to raise/hoist up' — 'you raised (him) up.'
segments.s019-g99: چو is the comparative/temporal particle 'as/like' (classical Persian also allows 'when' but here it introduces a simile). خورشید تابان is a noun + adjective 'the shining sun' (تابان = 'shining, radiant'). میان هوا is a locative phrase 'in the midst of the air/sky' (میان = middle/among, هوا = air/sky).
segments.s019-g100: نشسته is a participial/past form 'seated' used predicatively. بر او is the prepositional phrase 'upon him/it' (بر = on/upon, او = he/it). شاه فرمانروا is a nominal apposition 'the sovereign king' (شاه = king, فرمانروا = ruler/sovereign) — word order places the predicate 'seated' before the subject phrase, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s019-g101: جهان is the subject 'the world.' انجمن شد is a light-verb construction: انجمن (assembly, gathering) + شد (became) = 'was gathered/assembled.' بر آن تخت او is a locative phrase 'upon that throne of his' (بر = on/upon, آن = that, تخت = throne, او = his).
segments.s019-g102: شگفتی is a noun 'wonder, astonishment.' فرومانده is a compound participial/adjectival form meaning 'left behind/abandoned' or 'overcome' (here describing astonishment as being left/abandoned). از introduces the cause 'by/from,' بخت او = 'his fortune/fate' (بخت = fate/fortune, او = his). Grammatically this phrase comments on the effect produced by his good fortune.
segments.s019-g103: به جمشید is the indirect-object/benefactive phrase 'upon/for Jamshid' (به = to/for, جمشید = Jamshid). بر گوهر افشاندند is a verb phrase where بر گوهر means 'with/over gems' and افشاندند is the 3rd-person plural past of افشاندن 'they scattered/sprinkled' — together 'they scattered jewels upon Jamshid.'
segments.s019-g104: مر is an archaic/poetic particle here (not the modern 'me' form); it heads the nominal clause. آن روز را is the direct object 'that day' with را marking definiteness/accusative. روز نو is a noun phrase 'the New Day' (نو = new) and خواندند is the 3rd-person plural past of خواندن used in the sense 'to call/name' (they named it). The whole clause: 'they named that day the New Day.'
segments.s019-g81: This is a verb phrase with a preceding noun phrase: دگر + بوی‌های خوش = “other/another pleasant scents.” آورد is the 3rd-person past of آوردن (“brought”), and باز (here) is an adverb meaning “again.” The order (noun phrase before verb) is normal in Persian narrative and the whole chunk reads literally “brought other pleasant scents again.”
segments.s019-g82: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause (“because/for”); دارند is the present plural of داشتن (“have”) used here with the noun نیاز to mean “have need (of).” Note the poetic inversion: the verb دارند precedes its subject مردم. به بویش نیاز is a prepositional phrase “(have) need for its scent,” with یش the enclitic possessive “its/his/her.”
segments.s019-g83: This is a string of comparisons introduced by repeated چو/چون meaning “like” (similes). The conjunction و links items in the list. مشک ناب is a noun + adjective where ناب (“pure”) follows the noun as is common in Persian poetry: “pure musk.”
segments.s019-g84: Continuation of the list with the same repeated comparative particle چو before each item. روشن modifies گلاب and here means “clear/bright” rosewater; poetic word order may place the adjective before the noun for rhythm.
segments.s019-g85: پزشکی و درمان are two coordinated nouns (“medicine and remedy/treatment”). هر is the universal quantifier “every,” and دردمند is the noun “one in pain” or “sufferer”; together they mean “for every sufferer.” The underlying verb (is/are) is omitted as is typical in Persian nominal phrases.
segments.s019-g86: در is the preposition “in/for,” so در تندرستی = “for health” or “in health.” و links the next phrase; راه گزند is a nominal phrase meaning “a way/method (راه) of/against harm (گزند)” — i.e. a means to avoid or ward off harm.
segments.s019-g87: همان رازها is the definite noun phrase “those same secrets.” کرد is the simple past of کردن (“did/made”), and نیز is an adverb “also/likewise” modifying the action. آشکار is the result/complement meaning “manifest/clear” — so “he also made those same secrets manifest.”
segments.s019-g88: جهان را is topicalized with the object marker را after جهان (“the world” as topic/object). نیامد here is negative/past and is used idiomatically to mean “did not become / did not come to be,” and it takes the predicative phrase چنو خواستار (“such a desirous/asking one”) as its complement — i.e. “the world did not become such a seeker/desirer.”
segments.s019-g105: This is a temporal noun phrase meaning “at the head/start of the new year.” سر (literally “head/top”) is used idiomatically for the beginning; سال نو is “new year.” هرمز and فرودین follow as a proper name plus a time-name: Hormoz (Ahura Mazda) specified with the month Farvardin. The phrase functions as the time/setting for the clause that follows.
segments.s019-g106: Poetically the copula is omitted; this reads as ‘(he) was at peace.’ بر + آسوده gives the predicate “was at peace/on peace,” with بر used in a locative/secondary sense. از رنج is an ablative phrase “from suffering,” and روی زمین is a locative “on the face/surface of the earth.” The verb ‘to be’ is simply left out in this classical/poetic construction.
segments.s019-g107: بزرگان is the plural subject “the great ones/nobles.” به شادی is a prepositional phrase “in/with joy” modifying the verb, and بیاراستند is a literary past tense (3rd person plural) meaning “they adorned/arrayed (themselves or the place).” Word order is subject – adverbial – verb (typical in Persian poetry).
segments.s019-g108: This is a list of objects and the verb at the end: می‌و جام و رامشگران are the things/persons desired, with خواستند (3rd pl. past) ‘they wanted/desired’ as the governing verb. می‌و here (poetic) names the festive drink/fruit, جام is ‘cup,’ و is the conjunction, and رامشگران are people who bring calm/comfort (entertainers or peacemakers).
segments.s019-g109: چنین is a demonstrative/adverb “thus/so,” modifying جشن فرخ “fortunate/happy festival.” The adjective فرخ follows the noun as is normal in Persian poetry. از آن روزگار marks the provenance/time: “from that age/period.”
segments.s019-g110: به ما ماند is a dative construction meaning “remained to us” (ماند, past of ماندن, intransitive “remained/was left”). از آن خسروان یادگار is a genitive/agentive phrase “a memento of those kings.” Word order places the beneficiary (به ما) before the verb, which is typical in Persian.
segments.s019-g111: چنین سال سیصد is a temporal frame “thus (for) three hundred years.” The particle همی is an archaic/poetic continuous-aspect marker (“continually/kept on”), and رفت is the past tense “went/passed.” کار here names the prevailing affairs or activity (“affairs/work”), concluding the clause; the verb is centered on the passing/continuation of time and events.
segments.s019-g112: ندیدند is past tense 3rd plural of دیدن with the negative prefix ن-: “they did not see/encounter.” مرگ is the direct object “death.” اندر آن روزگار is a locative phrase “in that age/period.” The clause states that in that time they did not meet death.
segments.s019-g89: گذر کرد is a compound past tense: the noun گذر ('passage') + the past auxiliary کرد ('did/made') gives 'passed'. از آن پس is an adverbial phrase 'after that' (آن = 'that', پس = 'after'). به کشتی بر آب is a prepositional cluster: به کشتی ('by/onto a ship') + بر آب ('upon/over the water') describing the manner/location of passage.
segments.s019-g90: ز کشور به کشور is an idiom meaning 'from country to country' (ز = poetic 'from', به = 'to'). گرفتی شتاب is an older past construction: گرفتـی here functions as a past 'took' (archaic/poetic third-person form) and شتاب is the object 'haste' — together 'took haste / hastened'. Word order places the motion phrase before the verbal phrase.
segments.s019-g91: چنین سال = 'such/that year' (چنین qualifying سال). پنجه برنجید is the verbal clause: پنجه ('claw') is the subject and برنجید is a past verb meaning 'took offense / was angered' in poetic usage. نیز after the verb adds 'also/likewise'.
segments.s019-g92: ندید is past negative 'did not see'. The phrase از هنر بر خرد بسته چیز modifies the object چیز ('thing'): بسته is a past participle 'bound/closed', بر خرد 'against reason/wisdom', and از هنر indicates agency/source ('by/from skill'); together: 'a thing sealed by skill against (human) reason' — so the clause means 'saw no thing that skill had closed against wisdom.'
segments.s019-g93: همه کردنی‌ها = 'all the things to be done / all deeds' (کردنی‌ها is a nominalized plural). چو آمد = 'when it came' (چو = 'when'), and به جای = 'into place / in order' — together: 'when all the tasks came into place.'
segments.s019-g94: ز جای = 'from its place' (z = 'from'). مهی برتر is a compound adjective: مهی 'moon‑like / luminous' + برتر 'higher / superior', both qualifying پای ('pedestal/foot'). آورد is the past 'brought'. The noun پای (here 'base/pedestal') is the direct object — 'he brought forth a loftier moon‑bright pedestal from its place.'
segments.s019-g95: به فر کیانی is a dative/benefactive phrase 'for the Kayanian glory' (فر کیانی = 'Kayanian splendour/glory'). یکی تخت ساخت is straightforward: یکی ('one' / 'a certain') + تخت ('throne') + ساخت (past 'made/built') — 'he built a throne for the Kayanian glory.'
segments.s019-g96: چه مایه is an exclamatory quantifier 'what an amount / what a wealth'. بدو = 'to it' (dative pronoun). گوهر اندر = 'gems within' (گوهر 'gems', اندر 'in/within'). نشاخت is an archaic/poetic past verb here meaning 'set/inlaid' (the lemma list marks it as an archaic form). Entire phrase: 'what a quantity of gems he set into it.'
segments.s019-g41: This clause has a past-tense transitive verb بنشاندند (they seated/installed) in the 3rd-person plural. The phrase صفی بر دگر دست places the noun صفی (here the proper name Safi) as direct object/primary item, بر دگر دست = "on the other hand/side" (literally "upon the other hand"). Word order is object/phrase + verb, typical of Persian poetry: '[They] set Safi on the other side.'
segments.s019-g42: همی here is emphatic/adverbial (roughly 'thus/indeed') modifying the verb خواندند (they called/named). نام … خواندند is a standard construction meaning 'they called/named (someone) [by X]'; نیساریان is the name given. The verb is past 3rd-person plural, so 'they called them Nisarians.'
segments.s019-g43: کجا at the start functions locatively/exclamatorily ('there/where'); شیر مردان is a compound 'lion-men' = brave warriors. جنگ آورند is a present/habitual 3rd-person plural verb phrase ('they bring/engage in war' — i.e. 'they wage war'). The verb آورند is classical present with -ند.
segments.s019-g44: This is a nominal/copular clause: فروزنده (lit. 'illuminator, brightener') is predicate and لشکر و کشور is the domain it brightens. The final -ند on کشورند is the 3rd-person plural copula 'they are', so the whole means 'they are the brighteners of the army and the country/realm.'
segments.s019-g45: کز is the literary/poetic form of از + که meaning 'from which / from whom', here 'from them'. ایشان is the third-person plural pronoun. بود is the past copula 'was'. تخت شاهی is a genitive compound 'throne of kingship' (throne), and به جای is an idiom 'in its place' or 'firmly in position'. The clause reads 'From them was the kingly throne in its place.'
segments.s019-g46: و ز ایشان begins the second coordinated clause 'and from them'. بود again is past 'was'. نام here means 'reputation/renown' rather than a mere label, and مردی = 'of men' or 'manliness' (here 'men'). به پای is an idiom meaning 'standing/established' (literally 'at the foot/end'), so the clause means 'and from them the renown of men stood firm.'
segments.s019-g47: بسودی is the noun 'benefit/advantage' (here in the ablative/agentive sense 'by/through the benefit of'), سه دیگر = 'three other', and گره را شناس is an imperative/verbal phrase 'know the knot' (گره = 'knot' metaphorically 'difficulty/problem', را marks the definite object, شناس = 'know/recognize' or 'be acquainted with'). The whole is an instruction: 'Learn/recognize the knot by three other benefits.'
segments.s019-g48: کجا نیست is a negative existential idiom 'there is nowhere / there is not', followed by از کس بر ایشان سپاس literally 'thanks from anyone toward them'. Together: 'There is no thanks from anyone to them.' Note the prepositional sequence از کس (from anyone) + بر ایشان (to them) with سپاس as the noun 'thanks/praise'.
segments.s019-g1: This is a noun phrase in apposition: "گرانمایه" is an epithet placed before the proper name (literally 'esteemed/precious Jamshid'), and "فرزند او" is a postposed appositive meaning "his son" (او = he/him). Word order is adjectival epithet + name, then the relational phrase identifying him as someone's son.
segments.s019-g2: "کمر بست" is a past‑tense idiom meaning 'girded (his) loins / prepared' (کمر 'waist/girdle' + بست 'bound'). "یک‌دل" is an adjectival predicate/description 'single‑hearted' modifying the subject. "پر از پند او" uses پر 'full (of)' + از 'of' + پند 'counsel/advice' with او 'his' as possessor; together "full of his counsel." The clause is compact and elliptical (no explicit object for کمر بست because it's idiomatic).
segments.s019-g3: "برآمد" is a past intransitive verb 'rose/ascended'. It is followed by the locative/prepositional phrase "بر آن تخت" ('onto that throne'). "فرخ پدر" is a genitival/attributive sequence 'the fortunate (blessed) father' modifying تخت (the throne of the fortunate father). The overall order is verb + prepositional phrase + modifier describing whose throne it is.
segments.s019-g4: "به رسم کیان" is an instrumental/phrasing adverbial 'by/according to the custom of the Kayan (kings)'; here به functions as 'by/according to'. "بر سرش" is the locative 'upon his head' with the enclitic ش = 'his'. "تاج زر" is a noun + material ('crown [of] gold'), the verb is omitted (poetic ellipsis), so the clause implies 'a golden crown was set/placed upon his head.'
segments.s019-g5: "کمر بست" again is the idiom 'girded (his) loins / prepared.' "با" is the instrumental preposition 'with,' introducing the instrument/attribute. "فر شاهنشهی" is a compounded noun phrase: فر 'farr/royal glory' qualifying شاهنشهی 'imperial kingship' (an archaic/formal compound meaning the kingship of kings). Together: 'he girded himself with the royal glory of kingship.'
segments.s019-g6: Structure: subject "جهان" + verb "گشت" (became) + adverbial "سرتاسر" (throughout/entirely) + object/beneficiary "او را" (him, marked by را) + predicate noun "رهی" ('a road/way' used predicatively). The sense is idiomatic: 'the world became throughout a road for him' — i.e., the whole world was open to or served as a path for him. "گشت" = became/turned, and "رهی" here is used as a locative/predicative noun ('way').
segments.s019-g7: "زمانه" is the subject 'the age/era'. "بر آسود" is an archaic/periphrastic predicate meaning 'was at peace/was at rest' (بر + آسود). "از داوری" 'from judgment' indicates relief from judging or that judgment ceased; together: 'the age rested from judgment' (i.e., peace prevailed and formal adjudication was suspended).
segments.s019-g8: "به فرمان او" is an instrumental/agentive phrase 'by his command'. The following list "دیو و مرغ و پری" is a coordinated noun series (demon, bird, and fairy); there is no overt verb (poetic ellipsis), implying they obeyed or were at his disposal. The sequence thus states that, by his command, those beings were subject to him.
segments.s019-g121: یکایک is an adverb meaning “one by one / each in turn” placed before the verb for emphasis. به تخت مهی is a prepositional phrase: به + تخت (throne) with مهی post‑modifying تخت (literary adjective ‘moon‑like / shining’). بنگرید is the 3rd‑person singular narrative form (poetic b‑prefix + نگر) meaning “he looked / he gazed”; the subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s019-g122: به گیتی = “in/throughout the world” (prepositional phrase). جز از خویشتن را = an exception phrase: جز (except) + از (from/of) + خویشتن (himself) with را marking the definite/emphatic object. ندید is 3rd‑person past negative “he did not see.” Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (object/emphasis marked by را).
segments.s019-g123: منی کرد is a fixed expression meaning “he showed pride / acted arrogantly” (منی = arrogance/vainglory; کرد = did/made → ‘showed’). In the line the two-letter sequence (من + ی) is functioning as the noun 'منی' (classical/Arabic borrowing) rather than the pronoun 'I'. آن شاه یزدان‑شناس is the noun phrase “that king, God‑knowing / pious” modifying the actor. Grammatically, منی (object/nominal) + کرد yields the verbal action ‘became proud’ and is followed by the appositive subject phrase.
segments.s019-g124: ز یزدان = “from/because of God” (ز = from/of). بپیچید is a narrative/poetic past form (b‑prefix + پیچید) here meaning “he turned away / turned aside” (i.e. turned away from God). و شد ناسپاس = “and became ungrateful”: و = and/then, شد = became, ناسپاس = ungrateful/thankless. The clause states consequence: turning away from God produced ingratitude.
segments.s019-g125: گرانمایگان را = definite/direct‑object phrase “the illustrious/esteemed ones” with را marking the object. ز لشگر = “from the army/host” indicating source. بخواند is the 3rd‑person narrative/past form of خواندن here meaning “he summoned / he called (together).” Word order is object → source → verb, typical in poetry.
segments.s019-g126: چه مایه = an idiomatic phrase “what sort / what measure” (asking about degree or kind). سخن = “speech / words” (what to say). پیش ایشان = “before them / in their presence.” براند is the 3rd‑person form (b‑prefix + راند) used here as “he could put forward / utter / present” (literally ‘drive forth’) — the clause asks rhetorically what words he could offer to those men.
segments.s019-g127: چنین = “thus / so.” گفت here is the narrative past of گفتن “he said / he spoke” (introducing reported speech). با سالخورده مهان = “with the aged guests” (با = with; سالخورده = aged; مهان = guests). The whole segment sets up the reported clause that follows.
segments.s019-g128: که introduces the content clause (’that/which’). جز خویشتن را = “no one/ nothing except myself” (جز = except; خویشتن = myself; را = object/emphatic marker). ندانم is 1st‑person present/past negative (“I do not know / I know not”) derived from دانستن (here negative by prefix ن‑). جهان (the world) is the noun being commented on; word order is inverted for poetic effect: the speaker says he knows no world save himself.
segments.s019-g49: This is a coordination of three present (third‑person plural) verbs joined by و (and). The initial ب‑ in بکارند is the older/poetic present/telic prefix (equivalent to modern می‑), giving a third‑person plural form 'they sow/they cultivate'. خود is a reflexive/emphatic pronoun 'themselves' and is used before بدروند, here a plural present meaning 'they send/give greetings' (i.e., 'they greet one another'). Word order is Verb–و–Verb–و–(reflexive)Verb, a normal poetic listing of actions.
segments.s019-g50: به گاه is a time phrase 'at the time of / at the hour of'. خورش is the meal (stew) governed by that time phrase. سرزنش is a noun 'reproach' and نشنوند is negative present plural 'they do not hear' (from شنیدن); here it means 'they do not receive/are not given reproach'. The clause therefore states that at mealtime they are free of reproach.
segments.s019-g51: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (often marking separation or exemption). فرمان is 'command/order' and is the noun governed by ز. تن‌آزاده and ژنده‌پوش are compound nouns/adjectival compounds ('ascetic; one who disdains bodily comfort' and 'ragged‑clad') joined by و. The clause reads as an elliptic description: '(they are) from command — the ascetic and the ragged‑clad', i.e. 'the ascetic and the ragged‑clad are free from command'.
segments.s019-g52: Again ز = 'from'. آواز پیغاره is a genitive-like phrase 'the voice/cry of the messenger/notice' (آواز = voice; پیغاره = message/harbinger), and آسوده گوش is an adjectival phrase literally 'with an eased/resting ear' meaning 'untroubled in ear' or 'not attentive/worried by the call'. The prepositional ز governs the whole phrase, indicating exemption or freedom from the messenger's call.
segments.s019-g53: This fronted phrase gives the state that obtains 'for him' (بر اوی). تن آزاد و آباد گیتی is a coordinated nominal description: 'a free body and a flourishing world' — both predicates applying to the referent. بر + اوی is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'upon him / for him' (اوی is an archaic third‑person pronoun). Word order is descriptive phrase followed by the locative/prepositional marker.
segments.s019-g54: بر آسوده is a predicate 'is at ease' with بر echoing the previous locative 'upon/for' and آسوده 'at peace'. از introduces what he is free from: داور و گفتگوی, 'the judge and disputation' (two nouns joined by و). The construction means 'he is at rest, free from judge and debate'.
segments.s019-g55: چه گفت is the interrogative 'what said' (literally 'what was said' / 'what did he say'). آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying the following compound nominal سخن‌گوی آزاده مرد: سخن‌گو 'speaker/one who speaks' + آزاده (here 'noble‑hearted/free‑spirited') modifying مرد 'man'. Poetic order places these modifiers before/around the head noun; the whole phrase means 'that eloquent/noble man'.
segments.s019-g56: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'that/which'. آزاده را is the definite object marked by را ('a free man' as the object). کاهلی is the nominative/agentive noun 'laziness' and بنده is a predicative noun 'slave' (i.e., 'a slave/servant'). کرد is the verb 'made/caused' (causative/do), so the clause literally reads 'which laziness made the free man a slave' — i.e., 'which caused a free man to become enslaved by laziness.'
segments.s019-g33: ز هر انجمن پیشه‌ور گرد کرد = literal order 'from every assembly artisan around made' — here ز (preposition) introduces the source ('from'), هر انجمن = 'every guild/assembly' (modifier + noun), پیشه‌ور = 'artisan'. گرد کرد is the verbal phrase 'gathered' (گرد + کرد), where کرد is the past-tense auxiliary/doing verb and گرد here participates in the compound verbal sense 'to gather/assemble'. The object of گرد کرد is the artisans taken from each انجمن; the agent (he) is omitted as is normal in Persian past tense.
segments.s019-g34: بدین اندرون نیز پنجاه خورد = 'into/with this inner (group) also fifty fell/was allotted' — بدین (ب + دین) functions adverbially as 'by this / into this' (here 'to this inner circle'), اندرون = 'inner/within', نیز = 'also', and خورد is used in a poetic/idiomatic sense 'befell/amounted to/was allotted' (not literal 'to eat'). The clause states that fifty were assigned to that inner grouping.
segments.s019-g35: گروهی که کاتوزیان خوانی‌اش = 'a group that the Katuzians (کاتوزیان) خوانی‌اش' — grouping noun followed by a relative clause introduced by که. خوانی‌اش is a nominal/verbal noun + enclitic 'اش' (its) coming from خواندن; here it marks the name/recital by which the Katuzians refer to the group (poetic usage: 'what they call/recite for it'). The clause is elliptical: the relative clause identifies the group's name or how the Katuzians call it.
segments.s019-g36: به رسم پرستندگان دانی‌اش = 'by the custom of the worshippers you know it' — به رسم = 'according to/by the custom/rite', پرستندگان = 'worshippers/devotees', and دانی‌اش is the present 2nd-person singular verb دانی 'you know' with the enclitic -اش 'it' (you know it). The phrase tells the addressee they can recognize/know it from the worshippers' custom; دانی‌اش refers back to the group's name/reading mentioned before.
segments.s019-g37: جدا کردشان از میان گروه = 'he separated them from among the group' — جدا is an adverb/adjective 'apart', کردشان is a verbal construction: کرد (did/made) with the enclitic شان 'them' (object clitic), so together 'he separated them'. از میان گروه = 'from among the group/ranks' indicates the source. Note that the object clitic is attached to the verb (کردشان).
segments.s019-g38: پرستنده را جایگه کرد کوه = 'he made the mountain the worshipper's place' — پرستنده را marks the definite object/topic ('the worshipper(s)' with را), جایگه کرد = 'made (a) place/position', and کوه at the end is the noun placed in apposition/complement: 'made the mountain their place'. Word order is verb-final for the action (کرد) but places the complement (کوه) after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s019-g39: بدان تا پرستش بود کارشان = 'thus/thereby so that worship was their work' — here ب + دان (written as بدان/بدان) functions idiomatically 'by that/thereby' or 'thus', تا is a subordinating particle often 'so that' / 'until', پرستش بود کارشان = 'worship was their work' (بود is the past copula 'was'; کارشان = 'their task/affair' with possessive شان). The clause states purpose/result or condition: as a result, their occupation was worship.
segments.s019-g40: نوان پیش روشن جهاندارشان = 'youthful before their luminous sovereign' — نوان 'youthful/new' qualifies the people just mentioned; پیش here means 'in the presence of/before' (not just 'before' in time), روشن is an epithet 'bright/illustrious', and جهاندارشان = 'their world-ruler/sovereign' (جهاندار + possessive شان). The phrase is a descriptive clause placing them young/inexpert yet before their illustrious lord.
segments.s019-g73: چو is the comparative/simile particle 'like/as' placed before the nouns. گرمابه is a public bath (noun); و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking گرمابه to کاخ‌های بلند. کاخ‌های is the plural 'palaces' (کاخ + -ها) and بلند is the adjective 'lofty/high' following the noun in typical Persian order.
segments.s019-g74: چو again introduces a comparison ('as' / 'like'). ایوان is the noun 'iwan/portico'. که is a relative pronoun 'that/which' introducing the relative clause. باشد is an archaic/descriptive present-form of 'to be' here meaning 'is/serves as' (third-person form). پناه از گزند is the noun phrase 'refuge from harm' with the preposition از 'from' linking پناه to گزند.
segments.s019-g75: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. خارا here is the noun 'flint/rock' (source). گهر is the subject 'jewel/gem', and جست is the classical past of جستن 'leapt/sprang' (so the verb follows the subject). یک روزگار is a temporal phrase 'one day/for a while' modifying the action.
segments.s019-g76: همی is the older progressive particle meaning 'kept / was still' and here combines with کرد (did/made) to mark continuous action. از او 'from it' refers back to the flint (خارا). روشنی is the noun 'light', and خواستار is an adjectival/participial form meaning 'desirous/longing' — together 'روشنی خواستار' = 'seeking/longing for light.'
segments.s019-g77: به چنگ is a prepositional expression 'into the grasp/hand' (به + چنگ). آمدش is 'came to him' (آمد + enclitic -ش). چند گونه گهر is a noun phrase 'several kinds of jewel(s)' (چند = several, گونه = kind/type, گهر = gem), placed after the verb phrase to state what came.
segments.s019-g78: چو here introduces examples 'such as'. The list that follows is coordinated by repeated و: یاقوت is 'ruby', بیجاده is an obscure/rare term in this manuscript (here presented as a listed gem-name), and سیم و زر are 'silver and gold' respectively. The simple noun list is a typical poetic enumeration.
segments.s019-g79: ز خارا 'from the flint' (ز = from). به افسون is an instrumental phrase 'by/with enchantment' (به + افسون). برون is the adverb 'out/forth'. آورید is an older plural/past-form of آوردن 'brought', here functioning with the following شد to indicate that something was brought forth (the verbal cluster yields a passive/occurrence sense).
segments.s019-g80: شد is the verb 'became / came to pass' (here finalizing the action: 'was made / came into being'). آراسته modifies بندها: آراسته بندها = 'the adorned/ornamented fastenings/links' (بندها is plural). را is the definite/object marker attached before the final noun کلید 'key' — the word order is inverted for poetic meter, so literally 'became the key of the adorned fastenings.'
segments.s019-g129: This is a compressed, somewhat inverted Persian clause: 'هنر در جهان' (object/topic fronted) + 'از من آمد پدید' = 'came forth from me'. The verb cluster is poetic: the normal order would be 'پدید آمد' but Ferdowsi uses 'آمد پدید' for meter; 'از من' marks source.
segments.s019-g130: چو here functions as a comparative/introductory particle ('as / like / such that'). The clause is elliptical and poetic: it asserts that a 'تخت شاهی' (royal throne) 'نامور' (renowned) 'ندید' (was not seen) — i.e. there has been no throne as famed as mine. ندید is past (negative) used impersonally to mean 'was not seen / there was none seen'.
segments.s019-g131: جهان را is the definite object (را marks it); به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'with goodness / well'; من is the explicit subject, and آراستم is first-person singular past ('I adorned'). The word order (object + adverb + subject + verb) is topical/poetic but grammatically straightforward.
segments.s019-g132: This short clause states the result: 'چنان است گیتی کجا خواستم' means 'the world is such as I wanted.' چنان است = 'is such'; کجا here is used adverbially/idiomatically with خواستم ('I wanted') to mean 'as I wished.' خواستم is past first-person ('I wanted').
segments.s019-g133: خور و خواب و آرامتان is a coordinated list of nouns; آرامتان carries the second-person plural possessive -تان which by coordination applies to the earlier nouns as well ('your food/sleep/rest'). خور here functions as a noun meaning 'eating/food' (classical usage). 'از من است' is a prepositional phrase + copula: 'are from me / owe to me.'
segments.s019-g134: همان ('that same/the very') emphasizes identity; کوشش و کامتان is another coordinated pair where the possessive -تان on کامتان extends to کوشش as well ('your effort and your desire/satisfaction'). Again 'از من است' attributes their source to the speaker. آوَردن/آراستن style repetition is typical for emphasis.
segments.s019-g135: This nominal clause lists attributes that 'belong to me': بزرگی ('greatness') and دیهیم شاهی. دیهیم in older usage can mean 'of the people / rural' or 'popular'; شاهی is 'kingship/royal power'. مراست is an archaic fused form corresponding to 'مرا است' = 'is mine' (copula + enclitic).
segments.s019-g136: This is a rhetorical/relative question: the initial که introduces indirect speech/idea, گوید here is a literary present meaning 'would say / one would say' (the listed lemma senses do not reflect the verbal use, so sense is marked unknown). The clause 'که جز من کسی پادشاست' = 'that anyone except me is king' — i.e. rhetorical: who would say there is any king other than me? کسی is 'anyone/any person' and پادشاست predicates 'is king.'
segments.s019-g137: A noun phrase: همه (quantifier 'all/everyone') + موبدان (plural noun 'priests') followed by two post‑nominal adjectives سرفگنده نگون. In classical/poetic Persian adjectives often follow the noun; سرفگنده is a descriptive adjective 'head‑bowed/ashamed' and نگون is an archaic adjective meaning 'downcast/overthrown' modifying موبدان.
segments.s019-g138: An independent clause with interrogative/negated sense: چرا (here 'why') at the front, کس ('no one' in this context) + نیارست (an archaic past‑form meaning 'was not able / could not') + گفتن ('to say') and نه چون ('nor how'). Structure: 'چرا کس نیارست گفتن نه چون' = 'no one could say why nor how.' Note نیارست is an older poetic/compound form (negation + ability) rather than a modern analytic construction.
segments.s019-g139: A temporal clause: چو این گفته شد = 'when this was said', where گفته شد is a passive/periphrastic past ('it was said'). Then the main clause subject فر یزدان ('the divine/royal glory') followed by the prepositional complement از اوی ('from him') and the verb بگشت (an older past form of برگشت 'turned/returned') — i.e. 'the divine glory turned away from him.' اوی is an older enclitic pronoun meaning 'him'.
segments.s019-g140: Coordinated clause: بگشت و (it turned away, and) جهان شد پر از گفت‌وگوی. شد is the copular/becoming verb 'became', and the predicate 'پر از گفت‌وگو' is a common Persian pattern meaning 'full of conversation'. Word order: subject (جهان) + شد + predicate (پر از ...).
segments.s019-g141: Temporal clause introduced by چون ('when'): منی (here 'the self' / 'the ego' — formed with من + -ی) is the subject; بپیوست is the past form with the verbal prefix ب‑ meaning 'joined' (بپیوست = 'joined/was joined'); با کردگار = 'with the Creator' (با + کردگار). The clause means 'when the self joined with the Creator.'
segments.s019-g142: Two coordinated verbal phrases: شکست اندر آورد = 'brought defeat within/among (it)' — آورد is past 'brought/caused' with the object شکست preceding it (object‑verb order is common in older Persian), and اندر indicates 'into/within'. و برگشت کار = 'and the affair turned back/was reversed' where برگشت can be read as 'reversal' and کار 'the affair/work' (i.e. the situation was overturned).
segments.s019-g143: Interrogative frame starting with چه ('what / what did ...'). گفت here is the past‑tense verb 'said' (classical usage), followed by the subject آن سخن‌گوی ('that speaker' / 'the eloquent speaker') and the manner phrase با فر و هوش ('with glory and intelligence/wit'). The whole piece asks 'what did that speaker say, with splendour and wisdom?'.
segments.s019-g144: Reported content (imperative/advice): چو خسرو شوی = 'when/if you become king' (چو = 'when/if'; شوی is 2nd‑singular subjunctive/present of شدن 'to become' used in this conditional sense). بندگی را بکوش = 'strive for servitude/servitude (i.e. be humble)' where را marks بندگی as the object and بکوش is the 2nd‑singular imperative/subjunctive 'strive'. The sequence gives advice: 'When you become king, exert yourself toward servitude.'
segments.s019-g9: This chunk means “the world’s honour added to him.” Here جهان را is a poetic way to mark the possessor/thing concerned (literally ‘the world’ with the object marker را that links it to the following noun), فزوده is a past/participial notion ‘added/increased,’ بدو is the dative/indirect object ‘to him,’ and آبروی is the noun ‘honour/reputation’ that is being added.
segments.s019-g10: فروزان شده is a compound describing a change of state: فروزان (radiant) + شده (has become/has been made) = “has become radiant.” تخت شاهی is the noun phrase “royal throne” (تخت ‘throne’ modified by شاهی ‘royal/kingship’). بدوی is an archaic/adjectival qualifier meaning ‘original/primeval’ or ‘of old’ modifying تخت شاهی in poetic register.
segments.s019-g11: منم is the emphatic/copular ‘I am’ (or ‘I myself’) used to introduce the speaker. گفت here marks reported speech (‘he said’ / ‘said’), and با فره ایزدی is a prepositional phrase ‘with (or possessing) the divine farr’ (با = with, فره = the Zoroastrian/divine glory, ایزدی = divine). The phrase as a whole reports the speaker claiming divine glory.
segments.s019-g12: همم is the abstract noun ‘ambition/aspiration’ and is used twice in parallel for emphasis. شهریاری is the goal ‘sovereignty/kingship’ and موبدی is the other goal ‘mobad-ship / the priestly office.’ The construction is nominative/predicative: ‘my ambition (is) kingship, my ambition (is) mobadship,’ the copula being implied.
segments.s019-g13: بدان را is an archaic/poetic form meaning ‘to that one’ (بدان ≈ به آن) with را marking it as the affected/target. ز indicates separation or source ‘from,’ بد modifies دست giving ‘evil (bad) hand’ (دست here = power/powerful hand), کوته کنم is a first-person verb phrase ‘I will shorten/cut short’ (کوته ‘short’ + کنم ‘I make/do’) meaning ‘I will curtail [his] hand/power.’
segments.s019-g14: روان را is the patient ‘the soul’ marked by را. سوی روشنی is a directional prepositional phrase ‘toward the light’ (سوی = toward, روشنی = light/clarity). ره کنم is a verb phrase ‘I make/put on the way’ (ره = way/path, کنم = I cause/do), so together it means ‘I will lead/put the soul onto the path toward light.’
segments.s019-g15: نخست is the adverb ‘first/at first.’ آلت جنگ را is the object phrase ‘the instruments/weapons of war’ with را marking the definite object. دست برد is a verbal expression ‘took in hand / took up’ (دست = hand, برد here functions as ‘took’ in poetic narrative), so the whole segment means ‘first he took up the instruments of war.’
segments.s019-g16: در نام جستن is a fixed poetic expression ‘in seeking a name’ meaning ‘in pursuit of a name/fame’ (در = in, نام = name/reputation, جستن = to seek). به گردان سپرد is a verb phrase ‘he entrusted/handed over to the troop’ (به = to/into, گردان = troop/contingent, سپرد = handed/entrusted). Together: ‘seeking fame, he entrusted them to the troop.’
segments.s019-g17: به فر کیی نرم کرد آهنا — Word order: the prepositional/agent phrase (به فر کیی, “by/with the Kayanian glory”) comes first, followed by the verbal phrase نرم کرد (past of نرم کردن, “made/softened”). کرد is the past-tense auxiliary (‘made’); نرم is the adjective used with کردن to form the causative ‘to soften’. آهنا is an archaic/poetic form used as the direct object (see note).
segments.s019-g18: چو خود و زره کرد و چون جوشنا — چو before خود is the simile marker “like/as,” so چو خود reads “like himself.” و links clauses. زره کرد is ‘made (into) armor’ (زره + کردن). آخرِ جمله چو جوشنا is another simile: چون + جوشنا (“as/like boiling/aflame”), describing the armor’s appearance. The verb کرد is again the simple past ‘made’.
segments.s019-g19: چو خفتان و تیغ و چو برگستوان — This is a linked list introduced by the simile/collective marker چو (‘as/like’) before items; it names weapon-parts/arms (here: خفتان, تیغ, برگستوان). Each item is coordinated with و. In this line these nouns function as the things being displayed.
segments.s019-g20: همه کرد پیدا به روشن‌روان — همه is the quantified object/subject ‘all/everything’. کرد + پیدا corresponds to the compound پیداکردن (here in past: ‘made ... visible’ / ‘displayed’), with کرد as the past auxiliary. به روشن‌روان is a beneficiary/dative prepositional phrase ‘to/for the Bright‑Soul’ (a kenning/epithet).
segments.s019-g21: بدین اندرون سال پنجاه رنج — بدین اندرون is a temporal phrase ‘in this interval / within this time’ (بدین = ‘in this’), where اندرون ‘within’ locates the period; سال پنجاه is ‘fifty years’ (year + numeral) and رنج is the noun ‘hardship/suffering’. The whole segment says he bore/underwent fifty years of hardship.
segments.s019-g22: ببرد و از این چند بنهاد گنج — ببرد is here used as past/past-like ‘he bore/carried/endured’ (بردن family). و links clauses. از این چند = ‘from these (things) a few/several’; چند functions as ‘several/some’ and بنهاد (past of بنهادن) = ‘he placed/established/set aside’. گنج is the object ‘treasure(s)’. Together: from those years he put aside some treasure.
segments.s019-g23: دگر پنجه اندیشه جامه کرد — دگر here means ‘again/another’; پنجه اندیشه is a genitive or compound ‘the grip/clutch of thought’ (a metaphorical phrase), with پنجه ’grip’ + اندیشه ‘thought’. جامه کرد is ‘put on a garment’ (جامه + کردن: ‘to clothe/don’), so the line says he again put on the cloak/garment of thought.
segments.s019-g24: که پوشند هنگام ننگ و نبرد — که introduces a purposive/result clause here (‘so that’). پوشند is 3rd-person plural subjunctive/present form of پوشیدن ‘to wear/put on’ and expresses the intended/wished action (‘that they may wear’). هنگام = ‘at the time (of)’, and ننگ و نبرد list the nouns ‘shame (disgrace) and battle’ — i.e. the occasions when the garment is to be worn.

sentences.s019-s73: Whoever is ungrateful to God, fear comes into his heart from every side.
sentences.s019-s74: For Jamshid the day turned dark; that world‑illuminating glory continually waned.
sentences.s019-s29: Fourth, which they call ahtokhoshi — the very artisans' reluctance toward rebellion.
sentences.s019-s30: Where their work was the same as their fellows' craft, their minds were always full of thought.
sentences.s019-s31: In this way, within fifty years he also ate, labored, and gave things.
sentences.s019-s32: From these, each one chose a suitable station and showed a way.
sentences.s019-s57: There was no knowledge of their suffering and misfortune; the court (divan) stood closed in the middle like a wayfarer.
sentences.s019-s58: At the people's command he set both ears; the world was full of peace and filled with the sound of delight.
sentences.s019-s59: Thus it continued until this time came; they saw nothing from the Creator except goodness.
sentences.s019-s60: The whole world became entirely a path for him; the sovereign sat with nobility.
sentences.s019-s13: From linen and silk and the silkworm's hair he fashioned richly endowed brocade and fur.
sentences.s019-s14: He taught them to spin and to taft (a textile operation), and to weave the weft into the inner warp.
sentences.s019-s15: When the cloth was woven, they took up washing and sewing; they at once learned from him completely.
sentences.s019-s16: When this was done, he set another device in place; fortune favoured him, and he too was glad.
sentences.s019-s33: so that each person may see his own measure and know whether it is too little or too much.
sentences.s019-s34: Then he commanded the impure demon to mix the earth into the water.
sentences.s019-s35: Whatever came from the clay, when they recognized it, they formed it into light bricks.
sentences.s019-s36: The demon erected walls of stone and plaster; at first he worked on geometric carving.
sentences.s019-s49: That, when you wished, you took up a demon and raised him from the plain to the heavens.
sentences.s019-s50: Like the shining sun in the air, upon him the sovereign king sat.
sentences.s019-s51: The world became an assembly upon that throne of his; astonishment was left because of his fortune.
sentences.s019-s52: They scattered jewels upon Jamshid; they called that day the 'New Day.'
sentences.s019-s41: He again brought other pleasant fragrances, for people were in need of its scent.
sentences.s019-s42: Like ban and like camphor and like pure musk; like oud and like amber and like clear rosewater.
sentences.s019-s43: Medicine and remedy for every sufferer, for health and a way to ward off harm.
sentences.s019-s44: He likewise made those same secrets manifest; the world did not become such a seeker.
sentences.s019-s53: At the start of the new year, Hormoz (Ahura Mazda) in Farvardin was at peace, freed from suffering upon the earth.
sentences.s019-s54: The great ones arrayed themselves in joy; they desired wine and cups and sought peacemakers/comforters.
sentences.s019-s55: Thus that fortunate festival from that age remained to us as a memorial of those kings.
sentences.s019-s56: Thus three hundred years went by and affairs continued; they did not see death in that age.
sentences.s019-s45: After that he passed by ship over the water, hastening from country to country.
sentences.s019-s46: In that same year the claw was offended as well; it saw no thing that skill had sealed against reason.
sentences.s019-s47: When all the deeds came into place, he brought forth from its place a loftier, moon‑bright pedestal.
sentences.s019-s48: He fashioned a single throne for the Kayanian glory; what a wealth of jewels he set into it!
sentences.s019-s21: They set Safi on the other hand, and they called them the Nisarians.
sentences.s019-s22: There the lion-like men fought in battle; they were the brighteners of the army and the realm.
sentences.s019-s23: From them was the king's throne in its place, and from them the renown of men stood firm.
sentences.s019-s24: By three other benefits know the knot; there is nowhere any thanks from anyone to them.
sentences.s019-s1: The esteemed Jamshid, his son, girded his loins—single‑hearted and full of his counsels.
sentences.s019-s2: He ascended that fortunate father's throne; in the Kayan custom a golden crown was placed upon his head.
sentences.s019-s3: He girded himself with the farr of imperial kingship; the world became, throughout, a road for him.
sentences.s019-s4: The age was at peace with regard to judgment; by his command demon, bird, and fairy (were at his disposal).
sentences.s019-s61: He gazed one by one at the splendid throne; in the world he saw no one but himself.
sentences.s019-s62: That God‑knowing king grew proud; he turned away from God and became ungrateful.
sentences.s019-s63: He summoned the eminent men from the host, for what manner of speech could he place before them?
sentences.s019-s64: Thus he said to the aged guests: 'I know no world except myself.'
sentences.s019-s25: They sow and toil and greet one another; at the time of the stew they hear no reproach.
sentences.s019-s26: The ascetic and the ragged‑clad are free from commands; their ears are at rest from the messenger's voice.
sentences.s019-s27: For him the body is free and the world prosperous; he is at ease, free from the judge and from disputation.
sentences.s019-s28: What did that eloquent, noble man say that made a free man a slave to laziness?
sentences.s019-s17: He gathered artisans from every guild; fifty were also assigned to this inner circle.
sentences.s019-s18: A group which the Katuzians call (by that name) — you know it by the worshippers' custom.
sentences.s019-s19: He separated them from among the company and made the mountain their place (i.e., assigned the worshippers to the mountain).
sentences.s019-s20: Thus their task was worship; they stood youthful in the presence of their bright sovereign.
sentences.s019-s37: Like baths and lofty palaces, like porticoes that are a refuge from harm.
sentences.s019-s38: One day a jewel leapt from the flint; for a while it kept longing for light from it.
sentences.s019-s39: Several kinds of gems came into his grasp, such as ruby, (بیجاده), and silver and gold.
sentences.s019-s40: By charm they drew it out from the flint; the key of the ornamented fastenings was produced.
sentences.s019-s65: Art in the world came into being through me; no royal throne famed like mine has been seen.
sentences.s019-s66: I adorned the world with goodness; the world is exactly as I wished it to be.
sentences.s019-s67: Your food, sleep, and rest are from me; likewise, those same efforts and your satisfactions are from me.
sentences.s019-s68: Greatness and the people's (rural) kingship are mine; who would say that anyone other than me is king?
sentences.s019-s69: All the priests stood abashed and downcast; no one could say why or how.
sentences.s019-s70: When this was said, the divine glory turned away from him, and the world became full of conversation.
sentences.s019-s71: When the self joined with the Creator, it brought defeat within and the affair was reversed.
sentences.s019-s72: What did that eloquent speaker say, with glory and wit? — 'When you become king, strive for servitude (humility).'
sentences.s019-s5: To him the world had added honour; the royal throne of kingship became radiant.
sentences.s019-s6: He said, “I have the divine farr; my ambition is kingship, my ambition is the mobad’s office.”
sentences.s019-s7: I will cut short that one’s evil hand, and I will set the soul on the path toward the light.
sentences.s019-s8: First he took up the instruments of war in hand; seeking a name (fame), he entrusted them to the troop.
sentences.s019-s9: He softened the iron by the Kayanian glory here, and fashioned it into armor like himself and like a glowing furnace.
sentences.s019-s10: The scabbards and swords and bracers — he made them all visible to the Bright‑Soul.
sentences.s019-s11: In this time he endured fifty years of hardship, and from these he set aside several treasures.
sentences.s019-s12: Again he donned the garment of thought, so that they might wear it in times of shame and battle.
